![](_page_28_Picture_0.jpeg)

#### <span id="page-28-0"></span>*Requirement 2: Do not use vendor-supplied defaults for system passwords and other security parameters*

Malicious individuals (external and internal to an entity) often use vendor default passwords and other vendor default settings to compromise systems. These passwords and settings are well known by hacker communities and are easily determined via public information.

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                                                      | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Always change vendor-supplied<br>2.1<br>defaults and remove or disable<br>unnecessary default accounts<br>before<br>installing a system on the network.                                                   | Choose a sample of system components, and attempt<br>2.1.a<br>to log on (with system administrator help) to the devices and<br>applications using default vendor-supplied accounts and<br>passwords, to verify that ALL default passwords (including                                                               | Malicious individuals (external and internal to an<br>organization) often use vendor default settings, account<br>names, and passwords to compromise operating<br>system software, applications, and the systems on                                            |
| This applies to ALL default passwords,<br>including but not limited to those used by<br>operating systems, software that<br>provides security services, application<br>and system accounts, point-of-sale | those on operating systems, software that provides security<br>services, application and system accounts, POS terminals,<br>and Simple Network Management Protocol<br>(SNMP)<br>community strings)<br>have been changed. (Use vendor<br>manuals and sources on the Internet to find vendor-supplied                | which they are installed. Because these default settings<br>are often published and are well known in hacker<br>communities, changing these settings will leave<br>systems less vulnerable to attack.<br>Even if a default account is not intended to be used, |
| (POS)<br>terminals, payment applications,                                                                                                                                                                 | accounts/passwords.)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | changing the default password to a strong unique<br>password and then disabling the account will prevent a                                                                                                                                                     |
| Simple Network Management Protocol<br>(SNMP) community strings, etc.).                                                                                                                                    | 2.1.b For the sample of system components, verify that all<br>unnecessary default accounts (including accounts used by<br>operating systems, security software, applications, systems,<br>POS terminals, SNMP, etc.) are removed or disabled.                                                                      | malicious individual from re-enabling the account and<br>gaining access with the default password.                                                                                                                                                             |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                           | 2.1.c Interview personnel and<br>examine supporting<br>documentation to verify that:                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                           | •<br>All vendor defaults (including default passwords on<br>operating systems, software providing security services,<br>application and system accounts, POS terminals,<br>Simple Network Management Protocol<br>(SNMP)<br>community strings, etc.)<br>are changed before a system is<br>installed on the network. |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                           | •<br>Unnecessary default accounts (including accounts used<br>by operating systems, security software, applications,<br>systems, POS terminals, SNMP, etc.) are removed or<br>disabled before a system is installed on the network.                                                                                |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |

![](_page_29_Picture_0.jpeg)

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 2.1.1 For wireless environments<br>connected to the cardholder data<br>environment or transmitting cardholder<br>data, change ALL wireless vendor<br>defaults at installation, including but not<br>limited to default wireless<br>encryption<br>keys, passwords, and SNMP<br>community strings. | Interview responsible personnel and examine<br>2.1.1.a<br>supporting documentation to verify that:<br>•<br>Encryption keys were changed from default at<br>installation<br>•<br>Encryption keys are changed anytime anyone with<br>knowledge of the keys leaves the company or<br>changes positions.<br>2.1.1.b<br>Interview personnel and examine policies and<br>procedures to verify: | If wireless networks are not implemented with sufficient<br>security configurations (including changing default<br>settings), wireless sniffers can eavesdrop on the traffic,<br>easily capture data and passwords, and easily enter<br>and attack the network.<br>In<br>addition, the key-exchange protocol for older<br>versions of 802.11x encryption (Wired Equivalent<br>Privacy, or WEP) has been broken and can render the<br>encryption useless. Firmware for devices should be<br>updated to support more secure protocols. |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | •<br>Default SNMP community strings are required to be<br>changed upon installation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | •<br>Default passwords/passphrases on access points are<br>required to be changed upon installation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Examine vendor documentation and login to<br>2.1.1.c<br>wireless devices, with system administrator help, to verify:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | •<br>Default SNMP community strings are not used.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | •<br>Default passwords/passphrases on access points are<br>not used.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Examine vendor documentation and observe<br>2.1.1.d<br>wireless configuration settings to verify firmware on<br>wireless devices is updated to support strong encryption<br>for:                                                                                                                                                                                                         |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | •<br>Authentication over wireless networks<br>•<br>Transmission over wireless networks.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Examine vendor documentation and observe<br>2.1.1.e<br>wireless configuration settings<br>to verify other security<br>related wireless vendor defaults were changed, if<br>applicable.                                                                                                                                                                                                   |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 2.2 Develop configuration standards for<br>all system components. Assure that<br>these standards address all known<br>security vulnerabilities and are consistent<br>with industry-accepted system hardening<br>standards.<br>Sources of industry-accepted system<br>hardening standards may include, but<br>are not limited to:<br>•<br>Center for Internet Security (CIS)<br>•<br>International Organization for<br>Standardization (ISO)<br>•<br>SysAdmin Audit Network Security<br>(SANS) Institute<br>•<br>National Institute of Standards<br>Technology (NIST). | Examine the organization's system configuration<br>2.2.a<br>standards for all types of system components and verify the<br>system configuration standards are consistent with industry<br>accepted hardening standards.             | There are known weaknesses with many operating<br>systems, databases, and enterprise applications, and<br>there are also known ways to configure these systems<br>to fix security vulnerabilities. To help those that are not      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | 2.2.b Examine policies and interview personnel to<br>verify that<br>system configuration standards are updated as new<br>vulnerability issues are identified, as defined in Requirement<br>6.1.                                     | security experts, a number of security organizations<br>have established system-hardening guidelines and<br>recommendations, which advise how to correct these<br>weaknesses.<br>Examples of sources for guidance on configuration |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | 2.2.c Examine policies and interview personnel to<br>verify that<br>system configuration standards are applied when new<br>systems are configured and verified as being in place before<br>a<br>system is installed on the network. | standards include, but are not limited to: www.nist.gov,<br>www.sans.org, and www.cisecurity.org, www.iso.org,<br>and product vendors.<br>System configuration standards must be kept up to                                        |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | 2.2.d Verify that system configuration standards include the<br>following procedures for all types of system components:<br>•<br>Changing of all vendor-supplied defaults and elimination                                           | date to ensure that newly identified weaknesses are<br>corrected prior to a system being installed on the<br>network.                                                                                                              |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | of unnecessary default accounts<br>•<br>Implementing only one primary function per server to<br>prevent functions that require different security levels<br>from co-existing on the same server                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | •<br>Enabling only necessary services, protocols, daemons,<br>etc., as required for the function of the system                                                                                                                      |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | •<br>Implementing additional security features for any<br>required services, protocols or daemons that are<br>considered to be insecure                                                                                             |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | •<br>Configuring system security parameters to prevent<br>misuse                                                                                                                                                                    |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | •<br>Removing all unnecessary functionality, such as scripts,<br>drivers, features, subsystems,<br>file systems, and<br>unnecessary web servers.                                                                                    |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                              | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                       | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Implement only one primary<br>2.2.1<br>function per server to prevent functions<br>that require different security levels<br>from co-existing on the same server. | Select<br>a sample of system components<br>and<br>2.2.1.a<br>inspect the system configurations to verify that only one<br>primary function is implemented per server.                    | If server functions that need different security levels are<br>located on the same server, the security level of the<br>functions with higher security needs would be reduced<br>due to the presence of the lower-security functions.                                                       |
| (For example, web servers, database<br>servers, and DNS should be<br>implemented on separate servers.)                                                            | If virtualization technologies are used, inspect the<br>2.2.1.b<br>system configurations to verify that only one primary<br>function is implemented per virtual system component or      | Additionally, the server functions with a lower security<br>level may introduce security weaknesses to other<br>functions on the same server. By considering<br>the                                                                                                                         |
| Where virtualization technologies<br>Note:<br>are in use, implement only one primary<br>function per virtual system component.                                    | device.                                                                                                                                                                                  | security needs of different server functions as part of<br>the<br>system configuration standards and related<br>processes, organizations can<br>ensure that functions<br>requiring different security levels don't co-exist on the<br>same server.                                          |
| 2.2.2 Enable only necessary services,<br>protocols, daemons, etc., as required<br>for the function of the system.                                                 | 2.2.2.a Select<br>a sample of system components<br>and<br>inspect enabled system services, daemons, and protocols<br>to verify that only necessary services or protocols are<br>enabled. | As stated in Requirement 1.1.6, there are many<br>protocols that a business may need (or have enabled<br>by default) that are commonly used by malicious<br>individuals to compromise a network. Including this                                                                             |
|                                                                                                                                                                   | 2.2.2.b Identify any enabled insecure services, daemons,<br>or protocols and interview personnel to verify they are<br>justified per documented configuration standards.                 | requirement as<br>part of an organization's configuration<br>standards and related processes ensures<br>that only the<br>necessary services and protocols are enabled.                                                                                                                      |
| Implement additional security<br>2.2.3<br>features for any required services,<br>protocols,<br>or daemons that are<br>considered to be insecure.                  | 2.2.3 Inspect configuration settings to verify that security<br>features are documented and implemented for all insecure<br>services, daemons, or protocols.                             | Enabling security features before new servers are<br>deployed will prevent servers being installed into the<br>environment with insecure configurations.                                                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                                                                                   |                                                                                                                                                                                          | Ensuring that all insecure services, protocols,<br>and<br>daemons are adequately<br>secured with appropriate<br>security features makes it more difficult for malicious<br>individuals to take advantage of commonly used points<br>of compromise within a network.                         |
|                                                                                                                                                                   |                                                                                                                                                                                          | Refer to industry standards and best practices for<br>information on strong cryptography and secure<br>protocols (e.g., NIST SP 800-52 and SP 800-57,<br>OWASP, etc.).                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                   |                                                                                                                                                                                          | Note:<br>SSL/early TLS is not considered strong<br>cryptography and may not be used as a security<br>control, except by POS POI terminals that are verified<br>as not being susceptible to known exploits and the<br>termination points to which they connect as defined in<br>Appendix A2. |

|                                                                                                                                                    | 2.2.5.c. | Encrypt all non-console<br>2.3<br>administrative access using strong<br>cryptography.                                                              | 2.3 Select<br>a sample of system components<br>and<br>verify that<br>non-console administrative access is encrypted by<br>performing the following:                                                                              | If non-console (including remote) administration does<br>not use secure authentication and encrypted<br>communications, sensitive administrative or operational<br>level information (like administrator's IDs and                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |

| 2.6<br>Shared hosting providers must<br>protect each entity's hosted environment<br>and cardholder data. These providers<br>must meet specific requirements as<br>detailed in Appendix A1: Additional PCI<br>DSS Requirements for Shared Hosting<br>Providers. | 2.6<br>Perform testing procedures A1.1<br>through A1.4<br>detailed<br>in Appendix A1: Additional PCI DSS Requirements for<br>Shared Hosting Providers<br>for PCI DSS assessments of<br>shared hosting providers, to verify that shared hosting<br>providers protect their entities' (merchants and service<br>providers) hosted environment and data. | This is intended for hosting providers that provide<br>shared hosting environments for multiple clients on the<br>same server. When all data is on the same server and<br>under control of a single environment, often the settings<br>on these shared servers are not manageable by<br>individual clients. This allows clients to add insecure<br>functions and scripts that impact the security of all other<br>client environments; and thereby make it easy for a<br>malicious individual to compromise one client's data<br>and thereby gain access to all other clients' data. See<br>Appendix A1 for details of requirements. |

{35}------------------------------------------------

![](_page_35_Picture_0.jpeg)

### <span id="page-35-0"></span>**Protect Cardholder Data**

#### <span id="page-35-1"></span>*Requirement 3: Protect stored cardholder data*

Protection methods such as encryption, truncation, masking, and hashing are critical components of cardholder data protection. If an intruder circumvents other security controls and gains access to encrypted data, without the proper cryptographic keys, the data is unreadable and unusable to that person. Other effective methods of protecting stored data should also be considered as potential risk mitigation opportunities. For example, methods for minimizing risk include not storing cardholder data unless absolutely necessary, truncating cardholder data if full PAN is not needed, and not sending unprotected PANs using end-user messaging technologies, such as e-mail and instant messaging.

Please refer to the *PCI DSS and PA-DSS Glossary of Terms, Abbreviations, and Acronyms* for definitions of "strong cryptography" and other PCI DSS terms*.*

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Keep cardholder data storage to a<br>3.1<br>minimum by implementing data retention<br>and disposal policies, procedures and<br>processes that include at least the following<br>for all cardholder data (CHD)<br>storage:<br>•<br>Limiting data storage amount and<br>retention time to that which is required<br>for legal, regulatory, and/or<br>business<br>requirements<br>•<br>Specific retention requirements for<br>cardholder data<br>•<br>Processes for secure deletion of data<br>when no longer needed<br>•<br>A quarterly process for identifying and<br>securely deleting stored cardholder<br>data that exceeds defined retention. | Examine the data retention and disposal policies,<br>3.1.a<br>procedures and processes to verify they include the following<br>for all cardholder data (CHD)<br>storage:<br>•<br>Limiting data storage amount and retention time to that<br>which is required for legal, regulatory, and/or<br>business<br>requirements.<br>•<br>Specific requirements for retention of cardholder data (for<br>example, cardholder data needs to be held for X period for<br>Y business reasons).<br>•<br>Processes for secure deletion of cardholder data when no<br>longer needed for legal, regulatory, or business reasons.<br>•<br>A quarterly process for identifying and securely deleting<br>stored cardholder data that exceeds defined retention<br>requirements.<br>Interview personnel to verify that:<br>3.1.b<br>•<br>All locations of stored cardholder data are included in the<br>data retention and disposal processes.<br>•<br>Either a quarterly automatic or manual process is in place to<br>identify and securely delete stored cardholder data.<br>•<br>The quarterly automatic or manual process is performed for | A formal data retention policy identifies what data<br>needs to be retained, and where that data resides<br>so it can be securely destroyed or deleted as<br>soon as it is no longer needed.<br>The only cardholder data that may be stored after<br>authorization is the primary account number or<br>PAN (rendered unreadable), expiration date,<br>cardholder name, and service code.<br>Understanding where cardholder data is located<br>is necessary so it can be properly retained or<br>disposed of when no longer needed. In order to<br>define appropriate retention requirements, an<br>entity first needs to understand their own<br>business needs as well as any legal<br>or regulatory<br>obligations that apply to their industry, and/or that<br>apply to the type of data being retained. |
| | Sensitive authentication data includes the<br>data as cited in the following Requirements<br>3.2.1 through 3.2.3:                                                                                                                   | For all other entities, if sensitive authentication data is<br>3.2.c<br>received, review policies and procedures, and examine system<br>configurations to verify the data is not retained after<br>authorization.                                                                                           | perform, facilitate, or support issuing services to<br>store sensitive authentication data ONLY IF they<br>have a legitimate business need to store such<br>data.<br>It should be noted that all PCI DSS requirements<br>apply to issuers, and the only exception for<br>issuers and issuer processors is that sensitive<br>authentication data may be retained if there is a<br>legitimate reason to do so. |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | •<br>Database contents.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |

| •<br>One-way hashes based on strong<br>cryptography, (hash must be of the<br>entire PAN)<br>•<br>Truncation (hashing cannot be used to<br>replace the truncated segment of PAN)<br>•<br>Index tokens and pads (pads must be<br>securely stored) | •<br>One-way hashes based on strong cryptography,<br>•<br>Truncation<br>•<br>Index tokens and pads, with the pads being securely<br>stored<br>•<br>Strong cryptography, with associated key-management<br>processes and<br>procedures.                          | protected.<br>One-way hash functions based on strong<br>cryptography can be used to render cardholder<br>data unreadable. Hash functions are appropriate<br>when there is no need to retrieve the original<br>number (one-way hashes are irreversible). It is<br>recommended, but not currently a requirement, |
| •<br>Strong cryptography with associated<br>key-management processes and<br>procedures.                                                                                                                                                         | 3.4.b<br>Examine several tables or files from a sample of data<br>repositories to verify the PAN is rendered unreadable (that is,<br>not stored in plain-text).                                                                                                 | that an additional, random input value be<br>added to<br>the cardholder data prior to hashing to reduce the<br>feasibility of an attacker comparing the data<br>against (and deriving the PAN from) tables of pre                                                                                              |
| It is a relatively trivial effort for a<br>Note:<br>malicious individual to reconstruct original<br>PAN data if they have access to both the<br>truncated and hashed version of a PAN.                                                          | 3.4.c<br>Examine a sample of removable media (for example,<br>back-up tapes) to confirm that the PAN is rendered unreadable.                                                                                                                                    | computed hash values.<br>The intent of truncation is<br>to permanently remove<br>a segment of PAN data so<br>that only a portion                                                                                                                                                                               |
| Where hashed and truncated versions of<br>the same PAN are present in an entity's<br>environment, additional controls must<br>be in<br>place to ensure that the hashed and                                                                      | 3.4.d Examine a sample of audit logs,<br>including payment<br>application logs, to confirm that PAN is rendered unreadable or<br>is not present in<br>the logs.                                                                                                 | (generally not to exceed the first six and last four<br>digits) of the PAN is stored.<br>An index token is a cryptographic token that<br>replaces the PAN based on a given index for an                                                                                                                        |
| truncated versions cannot be correlated to<br>reconstruct the original PAN.                                                                                                                                                                     | If<br>hashed and truncated versions of<br>the same PAN are<br>3.4.e<br>present in the environment, examine implemented controls to<br>verify that the hashed and truncated versions cannot be<br>correlated to reconstruct the original PAN.                    | unpredictable value. A one-time pad is a system<br>in which a randomly<br>generated private key is used<br>only once to encrypt a message that is then<br>decrypted using a matching one-time pad and<br>key.                                                                                                  |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | The intent of strong cryptography (as defined in<br>the PCI DSS and PA-DSS Glossary of Terms,<br>Abbreviations, and Acronyms) is that the<br>encryption be based on an industry-tested and<br>accepted algorithm (not a proprietary or "home<br>grown" algorithm) with strong cryptographic keys.              |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | By correlating hashed and truncated versions of a<br>given PAN, a malicious individual may easily<br>derive the original PAN value. Controls that<br>prevent the correlation of this data will help ensure<br>that the original PAN remains unreadable.                                                        |

| 3.4.1 If disk encryption is used (rather<br>than file-<br>or column-level database<br>encryption), logical access must be<br>managed separately and independently of<br>native operating system authentication<br>and access control mechanisms (for<br>example, by not using local user account<br>databases or general network login<br>credentials). Decryption keys must not be<br>associated with user accounts. | 3.4.1.a If disk encryption is used, inspect the configuration<br>and observe the authentication process to verify that logical<br>access to encrypted file systems is implemented via a<br>mechanism that is separate from the native operating<br>system's authentication mechanism (for example, not using<br>local user account databases or general network login<br>credentials).<br>Observe processes and interview personnel to verify<br>3.4.1.b<br>that cryptographic keys are stored securely (for example,<br>stored on removable media that is adequately protected with | The intent of this requirement is to address the<br>acceptability of disk-level encryption for rendering<br>cardholder data unreadable. Disk-level encryption<br>encrypts the entire disk/partition on a computer<br>and automatically decrypts the information when<br>an authorized user requests it. Many disk<br>encryption solutions intercept operating system<br>read/write operations and carry out the<br>appropriate cryptographic transformations without<br>any special action by the user other than |
| This requirement applies in addition<br>Note:<br>to all other PCI DSS encryption and key                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | strong access controls).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             | supplying a password or pass phrase upon<br>system startup or at the beginning of a session.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| management requirements.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | 3.4.1.c Examine the configurations and observe the<br>processes to verify that cardholder data on removable media<br>is encrypted wherever stored.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | Based on these characteristics of disk-level<br>encryption, to be compliant with this requirement,<br>the method cannot:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | If disk encryption is not used to encrypt removable<br>Note:<br>media, the data stored on this media will need to be rendered<br>unreadable through some other method.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | 1)<br>Use the same user account authenticator as<br>the operating system, or<br>2)<br>Use a decryption key that is associated with<br>or derived from the system's local user<br>account database or general network login<br>credentials.<br>Full disk encryption helps to protect data in the<br>event of physical loss of a disk and therefore may                                                                                                                                                             |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | be appropriate for portable devices that store<br>cardholder data.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| 3.5<br>Document and implement procedures to<br>protect keys used to secure stored<br>cardholder data against disclosure and<br>misuse:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | 3.5<br>Examine key-management policies and procedures to verify<br>processes are specified to protect keys used for encryption of<br>cardholder data against disclosure and misuse and include at<br>least the following:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Cryptographic keys must be strongly protected<br>because those who obtain access will be able to<br>decrypt data. Key-encrypting keys, if used, must<br>be at least as strong as the data-encrypting key in                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| This requirement applies to keys<br>Note:<br>used to encrypt stored cardholder data, and<br>also applies to key-encrypting keys used to<br>protect data-encrypting keys—such key<br>encrypting keys must be at least as strong<br>as the data-encrypting key.                                                                                                                                                         | •<br>Access to keys is restricted to the fewest number of<br>custodians necessary.<br>•<br>Key-encrypting keys are at least as strong as the data<br>encrypting keys they protect.<br>•<br>Key-encrypting keys are stored separately from<br>data<br>encrypting keys.<br>•<br>Keys are stored securely in the fewest possible locations<br>and forms.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | order to ensure proper protection of the key that<br>encrypts the data as well as the data encrypted<br>with that key.<br>The requirement to protect keys from disclosure<br>and misuse applies to both data-encrypting keys<br>and key-encrypting keys. Because one key<br>encrypting key may grant access to many data<br>encrypting keys, the key-encrypting keys require<br>strong protection measures.                                                                                                       |

|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 3.5.1<br>Additional requirement for<br>Maintain a<br>service providers<br>only:<br>documented description of the<br>cryptographic architecture that includes:<br>•<br>Details of all algorithms, protocols,<br>and keys used<br>for the protection of<br>cardholder data, including key<br>strength and<br>expiry date<br>•<br>Description of the key usage for each<br>key<br>•<br>Inventory of any HSMs and other<br>SCDs used for key management | Interview responsible personnel and review<br>3.5.1<br>documentation to verify that<br>a document exists to describe the<br>cryptographic architecture, including:<br>•<br>Details of all algorithms, protocols, and keys used<br>for the<br>protection of cardholder data, including key strength and<br>expiry date<br>•<br>Description of the key usage for<br>each key<br>•<br>Inventory of any HSMs and other SCDs used for key<br>management | Note: This requirement applies only when the<br>entity being assessed is a service provider.<br>Maintaining<br>current documentation of the<br>cryptographic architecture enables<br>an entity to<br>understand the algorithms, protocols,<br>and<br>cryptographic keys used to protect cardholder<br>data, as well as the devices that generate, use<br>and protect the keys.<br>This allows an entity to keep<br>pace with evolving threats to their architecture,<br>enabling them to plan for updates as<br>the<br>assurance levels provided by different<br>algorithms/key strengths changes. Maintaining<br>such documentation also allows an entity to detect<br>lost or missing keys or key-management devices,<br>and identify unauthorized additions to their<br>cryptographic architecture. |
| Restrict access to cryptographic<br>3.5.2<br>keys to the fewest number of custodians<br>necessary.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Examine user access lists to verify that access to keys<br>3.5.2<br>is restricted to the fewest number of custodians necessary.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | There should be very few who have access to<br>cryptographic keys (reducing the potential for<br>rending cardholder data visible by unauthorized<br>parties), usually only those who have key<br>custodian responsibilities.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |

| 3.5.3<br>Store secret and private keys used<br>to encrypt/decrypt cardholder data in one<br>(or more) of the following forms at all<br>times:                                                                                                                                                    | 3.5.3.a<br>Examine documented procedures to verify that<br>cryptographic keys used to encrypt/decrypt cardholder data<br>must only exist in one (or more) of the following forms at all<br>times.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Cryptographic keys must be stored securely to<br>prevent unauthorized or unnecessary access that<br>could result in the exposure of cardholder data.<br>It is not intended that the key-encrypting keys be<br>encrypted, however they are to be protected<br>against disclosure and misuse as defined in<br>Requirement 3.5. | •<br>Encrypted with a key-encrypting key<br>that is at least as strong as the data<br>encrypting key, and that is stored<br>separately from the data-encrypting<br>key<br>•<br>Within a secure cryptographic device<br>(such as a hardware<br>(host)<br>security<br>module (HSM) or PTS-approved | •<br>Encrypted<br>with a key-encrypting key that is at least as<br>strong as the data-encrypting key, and that is stored<br>separately from the data-encrypting key<br>•<br>Within<br>a secure cryptographic device (such as a<br>hardware<br>(host)<br>security module (HSM) or PTS-approved<br>point-of-interaction device)<br>•<br>As<br>key components or key shares, in accordance with an<br>industry-accepted method                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| point-of-interaction device)<br>•<br>As at least two full-length key<br>components or key shares, in<br>accordance with an industry<br>accepted method<br>It is not required that public keys be<br>Note:<br>stored in one of these forms.                                                       | 3.5.3.b<br>Examine system configurations and key storage<br>locations to verify that cryptographic keys used to<br>encrypt/decrypt cardholder data exist in one (or more) of the<br>following form at all times.<br>•<br>Encrypted<br>with a key-encrypting key.<br>•<br>Within<br>a secure cryptographic device (such as a<br>hardware<br>(host)<br>security module (HSM) or PTS-approved<br>point-of-interaction device).<br>•<br>As<br>key components or key shares, in accordance with an<br>industry-accepted method.<br>3.5.3.c Wherever key-encrypting keys are used, examine<br>system configurations and key storage locations to verify:<br>•<br>Key-encrypting keys are at least as strong as the data |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | encrypting keys they protect.<br>•<br>Key-encrypting keys are stored separately from data<br>encrypting keys.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| 3.5.4<br>Store cryptographic keys in the<br>fewest possible locations.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | 3.5.4<br>Examine key storage locations and observe processes<br>to verify that keys are stored in the fewest possible locations.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Storing cryptographic keys in the fewest locations<br>helps an organization to keep track and monitor<br>all key locations, and minimizes the potential for<br>keys to be exposed to unauthorized parties.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | <b>3.6.3.b</b> Observe the method for storing keys to verify that keys are stored securely.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | encrypting key. | be securely archived (for example, by using<br>a key-encryption key).
