| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 6.4.5 Change control procedures must<br>include the following:                                                      | 6.4.5.a Examine documented change<br>control procedures<br>and verify procedures are defined for:<br>•<br>Documentation of impact<br>•<br>Documented<br>change approval by authorized parties<br>•<br>Functionality testing to verify that the change does not<br>adversely impact the security of the system<br>•<br>Back-out procedures | If not properly managed, the impact of system<br>changes—such as hardware or software updates<br>and installation of security patches—might not be<br>fully realized and could have unintended<br>consequences.                                                                                                              |
|                                                                                                                     | 6.4.5.b For<br>a sample of system components,<br>interview<br>responsible personnel to determine recent changes.<br>Trace<br>those changes back to related change control<br>documentation. For each change examined, perform the<br>following:                                                                                           |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| 6.4.5.1 Documentation of impact.                                                                                    | 6.4.5.1 Verify that documentation of impact is included in<br>the change control documentation for each sampled<br>change.                                                                                                                                                                                                                | The impact of the change should be documented so<br>that all affected parties can plan appropriately for<br>any processing changes.                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| 6.4.5.2 Documented<br>change approval by<br>authorized parties.                                                     | 6.4.5.2 Verify that documented approval by authorized<br>parties is present for each sampled change.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | Approval by authorized parties indicates that the<br>change is a legitimate and approved change<br>sanctioned by the organization.                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| 6.4.5.3 Functionality testing to verify that<br>the change does not adversely impact the<br>security of the system. | 6.4.5.3.a For each sampled change, verify that<br>functionality testing is performed to verify that the change<br>does not adversely impact the security of the system.                                                                                                                                                                   | Thorough testing should be performed to verify that<br>the security of the environment is not reduced by<br>implementing a change. Testing should validate that<br>all existing security controls remain in place, are<br>replaced with equally strong controls, or are<br>strengthened after any change to the environment. |
|                                                                                                                     | For custom code changes, verify that all<br>6.4.5.3.b<br>updates are tested for compliance with PCI DSS<br>Requirement 6.5 before being deployed into production.                                                                                                                                                                         |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| 6.4.5.4 Back-out procedures.                                                                                        | 6.4.5.4 Verify that back-out procedures are prepared for<br>each sampled change.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | For each change, there should be documented<br>back-out procedures in case the change fails or<br>adversely affects the security of an application or<br>system, to allow the system to be restored back to<br>its previous state.                                                                                           |

|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Upon completion of a significant<br>6.4.6<br>change, all relevant PCI DSS requirements<br>must be implemented on all new or changed<br>systems and networks, and documentation | 6.4.6 For a sample of significant<br>changes, examine change<br>records, interview personnel,<br>and observe the affected<br>systems/networks to verify that applicable PCI DSS<br>requirements were implemented and documentation<br>updated as part of the change. | Having processes to analyze significant changes<br>helps ensure that all appropriate PCI DSS controls<br>are applied to any systems or networks added or<br>changed within the in-scope environment.                                                                                                    |
| updated as applicable.                                                                                                                                                         |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | Building this validation into change management<br>processes helps ensure that device inventories and<br>configuration standards are kept up to date and<br>security controls are applied where needed.                                                                                                 |
|                                                                                                                                                                                |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | A change<br>management process should include<br>supporting evidence that PCI DSS requirements are<br>implemented or preserved through the iterative<br>process.<br>Examples of PCI DSS requirements that<br>could be impacted include, but are not limited to:                                         |
|                                                                                                                                                                                |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | •<br>Network diagram is updated to reflect changes.<br>•<br>Systems are configured per configuration<br>standards, with all default passwords changed<br>and unnecessary services disabled.<br>•<br>Systems are protected with required controls—<br>e.g., file-integrity monitoring (FIM), anti-virus, |
|                                                                                                                                                                                |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | patches, audit logging.<br>•<br>Sensitive authentication data (SAD)<br>is not<br>stored and all cardholder data (CHD) storage is<br>documented and incorporated into data<br>retention policy and procedures                                                                                            |
|                                                                                                                                                                                |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | •<br>New systems are included in the quarterly<br>vulnerability scanning process.                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |

| coding guidelines.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | common coding vulnerabilities.                                                                                                                                                     | Application developers should be properly trained to                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |

Having staff<br>knowledgeable of secure coding guidelines should<br>minimize the number of security vulnerabilities<br>introduced through poor coding practices. | for these requirements.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |                                                                                                                                                                                    | As industry-accepted secure coding practices<br>change, organizational coding practices and<br>developer training should likewise be updated to<br>address new threats—for example,<br>memory<br>scraping attacks.                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |                                                                                                                                                                                    | The vulnerabilities identified in 6.5.1 through 6.5.10<br>provide a minimum baseline. ![](_page_60_Picture_0.jpeg)

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Note:                                                                                                                                                               | Requirements 6.5.1 through 6.5.6, below, apply to all applications (internal or external).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Injection flaws, particularly SQL<br>6.5.1<br>injection. Also consider OS Command<br>Injection, LDAP and XPath injection flaws<br>as well as other injection flaws. | Examine software-development policies and<br>6.5.1<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that injection<br>flaws are addressed by coding techniques<br>that include:<br>•<br>Validating input to verify user data cannot modify<br>meaning of commands and queries.<br>•<br>Utilizing parameterized queries. | Injection flaws, particularly SQL injection, are a<br>commonly used method for compromising<br>applications. Injection occurs when user-supplied<br>data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command<br>or query. The attacker's hostile<br>data tricks the<br>interpreter into executing unintended commands or<br>changing data, and allows the attacker to attack<br>components inside the network through the<br>application, to initiate attacks such as buffer<br>overflows, or to reveal both confidential information<br>and server application functionality.                   |
|                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | Information should be validated before being sent to<br>the application—for example, by checking for all<br>alpha characters, mix of alpha and numeric<br>characters, etc.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Buffer overflows<br>6.5.2                                                                                                                                           | Examine software-development policies and<br>6.5.2<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that buffer overflows are addressed by coding techniques<br>that include:<br>•<br>Validating buffer boundaries.<br>•<br>Truncating input strings.                                                                   | Buffer overflows occur when an application does not<br>have appropriate bounds checking on its buffer<br>space. This can cause the information in the buffer<br>to be pushed out of the buffer's memory space and<br>into executable memory space. When this occurs,<br>the attacker has the ability to insert malicious code<br>at the end of the buffer and then push that malicious<br>code into executable memory space by overflowing<br>the buffer. The malicious code is then executed and<br>often enables the attacker remote access to the<br>application and/or infected system. |
| 6.5.3 Insecure cryptographic storage                                                                                                                                | Examine software-development policies and<br>6.5.3<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that insecure<br>cryptographic storage is addressed by<br>coding techniques that:<br>•<br>Prevent cryptographic flaws.<br>•<br>Use strong cryptographic algorithms and keys.                                        | Applications that do not utilize strong cryptographic<br>functions properly to store data are at increased risk<br>of being compromised, and exposing authentication<br>credentials and/or cardholder data. If an attacker is<br>able to exploit weak cryptographic processes, they<br>may be able to gain clear-text access to encrypted<br>data.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |

{61}------------------------------------------------

![](_page_61_Picture_0.jpeg)

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                        | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 6.5.4<br>Insecure communications                                                                                                            | 6.5.4 Examine software-development policies and<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that insecure<br>communications are addressed by coding<br>techniques that properly authenticate and encrypt all<br>sensitive communications.                                                      | Applications that fail to adequately encrypt network<br>traffic using strong cryptography are at increased<br>risk of being compromised and exposing cardholder<br>data. If an attacker is able to exploit weak<br>cryptographic processes, they may be able to gain<br>control of an application or even gain clear-text<br>access to encrypted data.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| 6.5.5 Improper error handling                                                                                                               | 6.5.5 Examine software-development policies and<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that improper<br>error handling is addressed by coding<br>techniques that do not leak information via error messages<br>(for example, by returning generic rather than specific<br>error details). | Applications can unintentionally leak information<br>about their configuration<br>or<br>internal workings, or<br>expose privileged information through improper error<br>handling methods. Attackers use this weakness to<br>steal sensitive data or compromise the system<br>altogether. If a malicious individual can create errors<br>that the application does not handle properly, they<br>can gain detailed system information, create denial<br>of-service interruptions, cause security to fail, or<br>crash the server. For example, the message<br>"incorrect password provided" tells an attacker the<br>user ID provided was accurate and that they should<br>focus their efforts only on the password. Use more<br>generic error messages, like "data could not be<br>verified." |
| 6.5.6 All "high risk" vulnerabilities identified<br>in the vulnerability identification process (as<br>defined in PCI DSS Requirement 6.1). | 6.5.6 Examine software-development policies and<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that coding<br>techniques address any "high risk"<br>vulnerabilities that could affect the application, as<br>identified in PCI DSS Requirement 6.1.                                               | All vulnerabilities identified by an organization's<br>vulnerability risk-ranking process (defined in<br>Requirement<br>6.1) to be "high risk" and that could<br>affect the application should be<br>identified and<br>addressed during application development.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Note:<br>(internal or external):                                                                                                            | Requirements 6.5.7 through 6.5.10, below, apply to web applications and application interfaces                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | Web applications, both internally and externally<br>(public) facing, have<br>unique security risks based<br>upon their architecture as well as the relative ease<br>and occurrence of compromise.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Cross-site scripting (XSS)<br>6.5.7                                                                                                         | 6.5.7 Examine software-development policies and<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that cross-site scripting (XSS) is addressed by coding<br>techniques that include<br>•<br>Validating all parameters before inclusion<br>•<br>Utilizing context-sensitive escaping.                 | XSS flaws occur whenever an application takes<br>user-supplied data and sends it to a web browser<br>without first validating or encoding that content. XSS<br>allows attackers to execute script in the victim's<br>browser,<br>which can hijack user sessions, deface<br>web sites, possibly introduce worms, etc.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                                          | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 6.5.8 Improper access control<br>(such as<br>insecure direct object references, failure to<br>restrict URL access, directory traversal, and<br>failure to restrict user access to functions). | Examine software-development policies and<br>6.5.8<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that improper access control—such as insecure direct<br>object references, failure to restrict URL access, and<br>directory traversal—is addressed by coding technique that<br>includes:         | A direct object reference occurs when a developer<br>exposes a reference to an internal implementation<br>object, such as a file, directory, database record, or<br>key, as a URL or form parameter. Attackers can<br>manipulate those references to access other objects<br>without authorization.                                                                                                                         |
|                                                                                                                                                                                               | •<br>Proper authentication of users<br>•<br>Sanitizing input<br>•<br>Not exposing internal object references to users<br>•<br>User interfaces that<br>do<br>not permit access to<br>unauthorized functions.                                                                                                        | Consistently enforce access control in presentation<br>layer and business logic for all URLs. Frequently,<br>the only way an application protects sensitive<br>functionality is by preventing the display of links or<br>URLs to unauthorized users. Attackers can use this<br>weakness to access and perform unauthorized<br>operations by accessing those URLs directly.                                                  |
|                                                                                                                                                                                               |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | An attacker may be able to enumerate and navigate<br>the directory structure of a website (directory<br>traversal) thus gaining access to unauthorized<br>information as well as gaining further insight into the<br>workings of the site for later exploitation.                                                                                                                                                           |
|                                                                                                                                                                                               |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | If user interfaces permit access to unauthorized<br>functions, this access could result in unauthorized<br>individuals gaining access to privileged credentials<br>or cardholder data. Only authorized users should be<br>permitted to access direct object references to<br>sensitive resources. Limiting access to data<br>resources will help prevent cardholder data from<br>being presented to unauthorized resources. |
| 6.5.9 Cross-site request forgery (CSRF)                                                                                                                                                       | 6.5.9 Examine software development policies and<br>procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify<br>that cross-site request forgery (CSRF) is addressed by<br>coding techniques that ensure applications do not rely on<br>authorization credentials and tokens automatically<br>submitted by browsers. | A CSRF attack forces a logged-on victim's browser<br>to send a pre-authenticated request to a vulnerable<br>web application, which then enables the attacker to<br>perform any state-changing operations the victim is<br>authorized to perform (such as updating account<br>details, making purchases, or even authenticating to<br>the application).                                                                      |

| 6.6<br>For public-facing web applications,<br>address new threats and vulnerabilities on an<br>ongoing basis and ensure these applications<br>are protected against known attacks by either<br>of the following methods:<br>•<br>Reviewing public-facing web<br>applications via manual or automated<br>application vulnerability security<br>assessment tools or methods, at least<br>annually and after any changes<br>This assessment is not the same as the<br>Note:<br>vulnerability scans performed for<br>Requirement 11.2.<br>•<br>Installing an automated technical<br>solution that<br>detects and prevents web<br>based attacks (for example, a web<br>application firewall)<br>in front of public<br>facing web applications, to continually<br>check all traffic. | 6.6 For public-facing web applications, ensure that<br>either<br>one of the following methods is<br>in place as follows:<br>•<br>Examine documented processes, interview personnel,<br>and examine records of application security<br>assessments to verify that public-facing web<br>applications are reviewed—using either manual or<br>automated vulnerability security assessment tools or<br>methods—as follows:<br>At least annually<br>-<br>After any changes<br>-<br>By an organization that specializes in application<br>-<br>security<br>That, at a minimum, all vulnerabilities in<br>-<br>Requirement<br>6.5 are included in the assessment<br>That all vulnerabilities are corrected<br>-<br>That the<br>application is re-evaluated after the<br>-<br>corrections.<br>•<br>Examine the system configuration settings and<br>interview responsible personnel to verify that an<br>automated technical solution that detects and prevents<br>web-based attacks (for example,<br>a web-application<br>firewall) is in place as follows:<br>Is situated in front of public-facing web applications<br>-<br>to detect and prevent web-based attacks.<br>Is actively running and up<br>to<br>date as applicable.<br>-<br>Is generating audit logs.<br>-<br>Is configured to either block web-based attacks, or<br>-<br>generate an alert<br>that is immediately investigated. | Public-facing web applications are primary targets<br>for attackers, and poorly<br>coded web applications<br>provide an easy path for attackers to gain access to<br>sensitive data and systems. The requirement for<br>reviewing applications or installing web-application<br>firewalls is intended to reduce the number of<br>compromises on public-facing web applications due<br>to poor coding or application management practices.<br>•<br>Manual or automated vulnerability security<br>assessment tools or methods review and/or<br>test the application for vulnerabilities<br>•<br>Web-application firewalls filter and block non<br>essential traffic at the application layer. Used in<br>conjunction with a network-based firewall, a<br>properly configured web-application firewall<br>prevents application-layer attacks if<br>applications are improperly coded or<br>configured.<br>This can be achieved through a<br>combination of technology and process.<br>Process-based solutions must have<br>mechanisms that facilitate timely responses to<br>alerts in order<br>to meet the intent of this<br>requirement, which is to prevent attacks.<br>"An organization that specializes in<br>Note:<br>application security" can be either a third-party<br>company or an internal organization, as long as the<br>reviewers specialize in application security and can<br>demonstrate independence from the development<br>team. |

{65}------------------------------------------------

![](_page_65_Picture_0.jpeg)

### <span id="page-65-0"></span>**Implement Strong Access Control Measures**

#### <span id="page-65-1"></span>*Requirement 7: Restrict access to cardholder data by business need to know*

To ensure critical data can only be accessed by authorized personnel, systems and processes must be in place to limit access based on need to know and according to job responsibilities.

"Need to know" is when access rights are granted to only the least amount of data and privileges needed to perform a job.

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Limit access to system<br>7.1<br>components and cardholder data to<br>only those individuals whose job<br>requires such access.                                                                                                                                                | Examine written policy for access control, and verify that the<br>7.1<br>policy incorporates 7.1.1 through 7.1.4 as follows:<br>•<br>Defining access needs and privilege assignments for each role<br>•<br>Restriction of access to privileged user IDs to least privileges<br>necessary to perform job responsibilities<br>•<br>Assignment of access based on individual personnel's job<br>classification and function<br>•<br>Documented approval (electronically or in writing) by<br>authorized parties for all access, including listing of specific<br>privileges approved. | The more people who have access to cardholder<br>data, the more risk there is that a user's account will<br>be used maliciously. Limiting access to those with a<br>legitimate business reason for the access helps an<br>organization prevent mishandling of cardholder data<br>through inexperience or malice.                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| 7.1.1 Define access needs for<br>each role, including:<br>•<br>System components and data<br>resources that each role<br>needs to access for their job<br>function<br>•<br>Level of privilege required (for<br>example, user, administrator,<br>etc.) for accessing resources. | Select<br>a sample of roles<br>and<br>verify access needs for each<br>7.1.1<br>role are defined and include:<br>•<br>System components and data resources that each role<br>needs to access for their job function<br>•<br>Identification<br>of privilege necessary for each role to perform<br>their job function.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | In order to limit access to cardholder data to only<br>those individuals who need such access, first it is<br>necessary to define access needs for each role (for<br>example, system administrator, call center<br>personnel, store clerk), the systems/devices/data<br>each role needs access to, and the level of privilege<br>each role needs to effectively perform assigned<br>tasks. Once roles and corresponding access needs<br>are defined, individuals can be granted access<br>accordingly. |
| Restrict access to privileged<br>7.1.2<br>user IDs to least privileges<br>necessary to perform job<br>responsibilities.                                                                                                                                                        | 7.1.2.a Interview personnel responsible for assigning access to<br>verify that access to privileged user IDs is:<br>•<br>Assigned only to roles that specifically require such<br>privileged access<br>•<br>Restricted to least privileges necessary to perform job<br>responsibilities.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | When assigning privileged IDs, it is important to<br>assign individuals only the privileges they need to<br>perform their job (the "least privileges"). For<br>example, the database administrator or backup<br>administrator should not be assigned the same<br>privileges as the overall systems administrator.<br>(Continued on next page)                                                                                                                                                          |

{66}------------------------------------------------

![](_page_66_Picture_0.jpeg)

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | 7.1.2.b Select a sample of user IDs with privileged access and<br>interview responsible management personnel to verify that<br>privileges assigned are:<br>•<br>Necessary for that individual's job function<br>•<br>Restricted to least privileges necessary to perform job<br>responsibilities. | Assigning least privileges helps prevent users<br>without sufficient knowledge<br>about the application<br>from incorrectly or accidentally changing application<br>configuration or altering its<br>security settings.<br>Enforcing least privilege also helps to minimize the<br>scope of damage if an unauthorized person gains<br>access to a user ID.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| 7.1.3<br>Assign access based on<br>individual personnel's job<br>classification and function.                                                                                                                                                                     | 7.1.3 Select a sample of user IDs and interview responsible<br>management personnel to verify that privileges assigned are<br>based on that individual's job classification and function.                                                                                                         | Once needs are defined for user roles (per PCI DSS<br>requirement 7.1.1), it is easy<br>to grant individuals<br>access according to their job classification and<br>function by using the already-created roles.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| Require documented<br>7.1.4<br>approval by authorized parties<br>specifying required privileges.                                                                                                                                                                  | 7.1.4 Select a sample of user IDs and compare with documented<br>approvals to verify that:<br>•<br>Documented approval exists for the assigned privileges<br>•<br>The approval was by authorized parties<br>•<br>That specified privileges match the roles assigned to the<br>individual.         | Documented approval (for example, in writing or<br>electronically) assures that those with access and<br>privileges are known and authorized by<br>management, and that their access is necessary for<br>their job function.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| Establish an access control<br>7.2<br>system(s)<br>for systems components<br>that restricts access based on a<br>user's need to know, and is set to<br>"deny all" unless specifically allowed.<br>This access control system(s)<br>must<br>include the following: | Examine system settings and vendor documentation to verify<br>7.2<br>that an access control system(s)<br>is implemented as follows:                                                                                                                                                               | Without a mechanism to restrict access based on<br>user's need to know, a user may unknowingly be<br>granted access to cardholder data. Access control<br>systems<br>automate the process of restricting access<br>and assigning privileges. Additionally, a default<br>"deny-all" setting ensures no one is granted access<br>until and unless a rule is established specifically<br>granting such access.<br>Entities may have one or<br>more access controls systems to manage user<br>access.<br>Note:<br>Some access control systems are set by<br>default to "allow-all," thereby permitting access<br>unless/until a rule is written to specifically deny it. |
| Coverage of all system<br>7.2.1<br>components                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | Confirm that access control systems are in place on all<br>7.2.1<br>system components.                                                                                                                                                                                                            |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| 7.2.2 Assignment of privileges to<br>individuals based on job<br>classification and function.                                                                                                                                                                     | 7.2.2 Confirm that access control systems are configured to<br>enforce privileges assigned to individuals based on job<br>classification and function.                                                                                                                                            |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| 7.2.3<br>Default "deny-all" setting.                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | 7.2.3<br>Confirm that the access control systems have a default<br>"deny-all" setting.                                                                                                                                                                                                            |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

{67}------------------------------------------------

![](_page_67_Picture_0.jpeg)

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                             | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 7.3<br>Ensure that security policies and<br>operational procedures for restricting<br>access to cardholder data are<br>documented, in use, and known to<br>all affected parties. | 7.3<br>Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that<br>security policies and operational procedures for restricting access<br>to cardholder data are:<br>•<br>Documented,<br>•<br>In use, and<br>•<br>Known<br>to all affected parties. | Personnel need to be aware of and following<br>security policies and operational procedures to<br>ensure that access is controlled and based on need<br>to-know and least privilege, on a continuous basis. |

{68}------------------------------------------------

![](_page_68_Picture_0.jpeg)

#### <span id="page-68-0"></span>*Requirement 8: Identify and authenticate access to system components*

Assigning a unique identification (ID) to each person with access ensures that each individual is uniquely accountable for their actions. When such accountability is in place, actions taken on critical data and systems are performed by, and can be traced to, known and authorized users and processes.

The effectiveness of a password is largely determined by the design and implementation of the authentication system—particularly, how frequently password attempts can be made by an attacker, and the security methods to protect user passwords at the point of entry, during transmission, and while in storage.

*Note: These requirements are applicable for all accounts, including point-of-sale accounts, with administrative capabilities and all accounts used to view or access cardholder data or to access systems with cardholder data. This includes accounts used by vendors and other third parties (for example, for support or maintenance). These requirements do not apply to accounts used by consumers (e.g., cardholders).* 

*However, Requirements 8.1.1, 8.2, 8.5, 8.2.3 through 8.2.5, and 8.1.6 through 8.1.8 are not intended to apply to user accounts within a point-ofsale payment application that only have access to one card number at a time in order to facilitate a single transaction (such as cashier accounts).*

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                              | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 8.1<br>Define and implement policies and<br>procedures to ensure proper user                                      | 8.1.a<br>Review procedures and confirm they define processes for<br>each of the items below at 8.1.1 through 8.1.8                                                                                                                                                          | By ensuring each user is uniquely identified—<br>instead of using one ID for several employees—an                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| identification management for non<br>consumer users and<br>administrators on all<br>system components as follows: | 8.1.b Verify that procedures are implemented for user<br>identification management, by performing the following:                                                                                                                                                            | organization can maintain individual responsibility<br>for actions and an effective audit trail per employee.<br>This will help speed issue resolution and                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| 8.1.1 Assign all users a unique ID<br>before allowing them to access system<br>components or cardholder data.     | 8.1.1 Interview administrative personnel to confirm that all<br>users are assigned a unique ID for access to system<br>components or cardholder data.                                                                                                                       | containment when misuse or malicious intent<br>occurs.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| 8.1.2 Control addition, deletion, and<br>modification of user IDs, credentials,<br>and other identifier objects.  | 8.1.2 For a sample of privileged user IDs and general user IDs,<br>examine associated authorizations and observe system<br>settings to verify each user ID and privileged user ID has been<br>implemented with only the privileges specified on the<br>documented approval. | To ensure that user accounts granted access to<br>systems are all valid and recognized users, strong<br>processes must manage all changes to user IDs and<br>other authentication credentials, including adding<br>new ones and modifying or deleting existing ones.                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| 8.1.3 Immediately revoke access for<br>any terminated users.                                                      | Select a sample of users terminated in the past six<br>8.1.3.a<br>months, and review current user access lists—for<br>both<br>local<br>and remote access—to verify that their IDs have been<br>deactivated or removed from the<br>access lists.                             | If an employee has left the company and still has<br>access to the network via their user account,<br>unnecessary or malicious access to cardholder data<br>could occur—either by the former employee or by<br>a<br>malicious user who exploits the old and/or unused<br>account. To prevent unauthorized access, user<br>credentials and other authentication methods<br>therefore need to<br>be revoked promptly (as soon as<br>possible) upon the employee's<br>departure. |
|                                                                                                                   | Verify all physical authentication methods—such as,<br>8.1.3.b<br>smart cards, tokens, etc.—have been returned or deactivated.                                                                                                                                              |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

![](_page_69_Picture_0.jpeg)

| PCI DSS Requirements                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | Testing Procedures                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | Guidance                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 8.1.4 Remove/disable inactive user<br>accounts within 90 days.                                                                                                                                                                                           | 8.1.4 Observe user accounts to verify that any inactive<br>accounts over 90 days old are either removed or disabled.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | Accounts that are not used regularly are often<br>targets of attack since it is less likely that any<br>changes (such as a changed password) will be<br>noticed. As such, these accounts may be more<br>easily exploited and used to access cardholder data.                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| 8.1.5 Manage<br>IDs used by third parties<br>to access, support, or maintain system<br>components via remote access as<br>follows:<br>•<br>Enabled only during the time<br>period needed and disabled when<br>not in use.<br>•<br>Monitored when in use. | 8.1.5.a Interview personnel and observe processes for<br>managing accounts<br>used by third parties<br>to access, support, or<br>maintain system components to verify that accounts used for<br>remote access are:<br>•<br>Disabled when not in use<br>•<br>Enabled only when needed by the third party, and<br>disabled when not in use.<br>8.1.5.b Interview personnel and observe processes to verify | Allowing vendors to have 24/7 access into your<br>network in case they need to support your systems<br>increases the chances of unauthorized access,<br>either from a user in the vendor's environment or<br>from a malicious individual who finds and uses this<br>always-available external entry point into your<br>network. Enabling access only for the time periods<br>needed, and disabling it<br>as soon as it is no longer<br>needed, helps prevent misuse of these connections. |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | that third-party<br>remote access accounts are monitored while<br>being used.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Monitoring of vendor access provides assurance<br>that vendors are accessing only the systems<br>necessary and only during approved time frames.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Limit repeated access attempts<br>8.1.6<br>by locking out the user ID after not<br>more than six attempts.                                                                                                                                               | For a sample of system components, inspect system<br>8.1.6.a<br>configuration settings to verify that authentication parameters<br>are set to require that user accounts be<br>locked out after not<br>more than six invalid logon attempts.                                                                                                                                                             | Without account-lockout mechanisms in place, an<br>attacker can continually attempt to guess a<br>password through manual or automated tools (for<br>example, password cracking), until they achieve<br>success and gain access to a user's account.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | 8.1.6.b<br>Additional testing procedure for service provider<br>assessments only:<br>Review<br>internal processes and<br>customer/user documentation, and observe implemented<br>processes to verify that non-consumer customer user accounts<br>are temporarily locked-out after not more than six invalid<br>access attempts.                                                                          | Testing Procedure 8.1.6.b is an additional<br>Note:<br>procedure that only applies if the entity being<br>assessed is a service provider.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Set the lockout duration to a<br>8.1.7<br>minimum of 30 minutes or until an<br>administrator enables the user ID.                                                                                                                                        | For a sample of system components, inspect system<br>8.1.7<br>configuration settings to verify that password parameters are<br>set to require that once a user account is locked out, it remains<br>locked for a minimum of 30 minutes or until a system<br>administrator resets the account.                                                                                                            | If an account is locked out due to someone<br>continually trying to guess a password, controls to<br>delay reactivation of these locked accounts stops<br>the malicious individual from continually guessing<br>the password (they will have to stop for a minimum<br>of 30 minutes until the account is reactivated).<br>Additionally, if reactivation must be requested, the<br>admin or help desk can validate that it is the actual<br>account owner requesting reactivation.         |
